title: Get Help Filing Your Citizenship Application, March 15

Staff and volunteer attorneys will help you complete the N-400 form, review your documents, and answer questions about the naturalization process. Interpretation available in Spanish, Somali, Hmong, Vietnamese, and Karen.

What to bring: your green card, a government-issued photo ID, any previous immigration documents, and two passport photos.

Space is limited to 40 participants. Register by March 11 at iimn.org or call 651-647-0191.

title: Food Shelves Need More Hands as Demand Surges
bodyRaw:
Client visits at CEAP's food shelf in Brooklyn Center have climbed steadily for three years running, and the volunteer crew that sorts, stocks, and distributes is stretched thin. They need people who can commit to a regular weekly or biweekly shift: sorting donated food, restocking shelves, and helping families navigate the client-choice shopping floor.

Shifts run Monday through Friday during food shelf hours. No experience required. You should be able to stand for a few hours and lift grocery-weight boxes. If you can commit to the same slot each week, even better, because consistency matters when the team is small. Contact CEAP's volunteer coordinator at **763-566-9600** or stop by 6840 78th Ave N, Brooklyn Center, and ask for the volunteer desk.

title: Spring Toy Drive Starts Soon: Drop Off Donations
bodyRaw:
The holiday season proved that families show up for kids when you make it easy. Now STEP is getting ahead of spring. Their annual spring toy and children's supply drive begins in March, collecting new toys, books, art supplies, and outdoor gear for kids in St. Louis Park and surrounding communities.

Drop off donations at **6812 W Lake St, St. Louis Park**, Tuesday and Thursday from 9am to noon, or Saturday mornings from 9am to 11am beginning March 8. New items only; no used toys, please. If you want to organize a drive at your workplace or school, contact STEP at **952-928-6082** and they will give you a collection bin and a flyer.

title: Vietnamese Interpreters Needed for Health Appointments
bodyRaw:
Vietnamese Social Services of Minnesota (VSSMN) is looking for bilingual Vietnamese-English volunteers to accompany community members to medical and dental appointments in the St. Paul area. Most appointments are weekday mornings.

Time commitment: 2–4 hours per month. You do not need formal interpreter training; VSSMN will provide orientation. Volunteers must be 18+, comfortable in clinical settings, and available for a brief intake call.

This is meaningful, hands-on work. Many VSSMN clients are elderly and navigate appointments alone. Your presence changes how those visits go.

Contact: Tuyen Nguyen, 651-472-7979 or info@vssmn.org.

title: Weekday Volunteers Needed at Shakopee Food Shelf
bodyRaw:
CAP Agency's Scott County food shelf needs 15 to 20 volunteers per week to keep up with growing demand. Shifts are Tuesday through Friday, 8:30 a.m. to noon.

Tasks include unloading deliveries, sorting donations, stocking shelves, and welcoming clients. No experience required; training takes about 20 minutes. Background checks are not required for general food shelf shifts.

HOME Line's tenant counselors will explain what happens at an eviction hearing, help you understand what defenses you may have, and tell you what to bring. They cannot represent you in court, but they can make sure you walk in prepared.

If your hearing is less than seven days away, say that when you call, and they will prioritize your case. The hotline is at **612-728-5767**, Monday through Friday, 9am to 4:30pm. Free. No ID required. Spanish and Somali interpretation available.

title: Know Your Rights if ICE Comes to Your Door
bodyRaw:
The Immigrant Law Center of Minnesota wants every family to know four things before opening their door:

1. You do not have to open the door for ICE unless they have a judicial warrant signed by a judge. An administrative warrant is not enough.
2. You have the right to remain silent. You do not have to answer questions about your immigration status.
3. Do not sign anything without speaking to a lawyer first.
4. Call the ILCM emergency line immediately: 612-358-0111.

ILCM is offering free Know Your Rights sessions every Saturday in March at their office at 450 N. Syndicate St., St. Paul (Room 200). Sessions run 10am–noon. No appointment needed. Spanish, Hmong, Somali, and Karen interpretation will be available on-site.

title: Youth Shelters Have Beds: Spread the Word
bodyRaw:
If you know a young person between 16 and 24 who is sleeping on couches, in cars, or outside, there are shelter beds available right now in Hennepin County. Simpson Housing Services runs transitional housing programs specifically for young adults, with case management, job readiness support, and help finding a permanent place to live.

The hardest part for most young people is making the first call. Many don't think of themselves as homeless; they're just "staying with a friend for a while." If you are an adult in a young person's life and you suspect they don't have a stable place to sleep, share this number: **612-874-8683**. Simpson's intake team can talk through options without pressure. Emergency drop-in at **2740 1st Ave S, Minneapolis**.

title: Deb Hartmann Has Run New Prague's Food Shelf for 11 Years

person.name: Deb Hartmann
person.bio: Deb has run the Peace Center Food Shelf in New Prague for 11 years, growing it from a twice-monthly distribution to a full weekly operation with a mobile pantry and 30 regular volunteers.
person.quote: The hardest part isn't the logistics. It's getting people to walk through the door the first time.

bodyRaw:
Deb Hartmann started volunteering at the Peace Center Food Shelf the year her youngest started high school. She became coordinator two years later and hasn't looked back.

Under her watch, the shelf grew from a twice-monthly distribution to a full weekly operation with a mobile pantry component. She added fresh produce sourced from area farms, secured a relationship with a regional food bank, and recruited a core of 30 regular volunteers.

"The hardest part isn't the logistics," she said. "It's getting people to walk through the door the first time. There's still so much shame around needing help. Our job is to make this feel like a normal place to be."

The Peace Center serves New Prague, Montgomery, Lonsdale, and surrounding townships. Deb trains all new volunteers herself.

title: Destiny Beat the Odds. Now She's Serving Her Country

person.name: Destiny
person.bio: Destiny grew up in Twin Cities poverty, couch-surfed through high school, graduated on time, earned honors at Spelman College, and completed law school in 2025. She is now a JAG officer in the U.S. Air Force.
person.quote: I didn't need someone to fix everything. I needed one person to say: you can do this.

bodyRaw:
Destiny does not talk about her childhood like it was a tragedy. She talks about it like a set of facts she had to work around. A mother whose mental health made home unpredictable. A junior year that started on a friend's couch in St. Paul after her mother left the state. Months of not knowing where she would sleep next week.

What she does talk about is what changed things: a youth shelter that gave her a stable address. A transitional housing program that let her stay enrolled in school. Adults who showed up consistently—caseworkers, teachers, a shelter volunteer who drove her to a college visit.

She graduated high school on time. Earned a degree from Spelman College cum laude. Completed law school in the Twin Cities and accepted a commission as a JAG officer in the U.S. Air Force. Catholic Charities Twin Cities provided housing navigation during a critical stretch of her transition. Their intake line is **651-232-5428**.

title: Keystone's Pantry Manager Has Fed This Block for 14 Years

person.name: Marcus Osei
person.bio: Marcus Osei has managed the Keystone Community Food Center since 2012. He championed a client-choice model that lets visitors select their own groceries, and expanded service to four Midway apartment complexes via a mobile pantry.
person.quote: People have allergies. People have preferences. People have dignity. You don't hand someone a random box and call it help.

bodyRaw:
Marcus Osei started volunteering at Keystone Community Services' Community Food Center the year he moved to St. Paul. That was 2012. Today he manages the pantry full-time, overseeing a team of 30 volunteers who keep the shelves stocked at 1090 University Ave. W.

The Keystone food center serves roughly 600 households a month, operating Monday through Friday, 9am–4pm. Unlike many food shelves, Keystone uses a client-choice model where visitors walk the aisles and select what they want, like a small grocery store. Osei pushed hard for the switch eight years ago.

"People have allergies. People have preferences. People have dignity," he says. "You don't hand someone a random box and call it help."

Keystone also runs a mobile pantry that visits four apartment complexes in the Midway neighborhood on alternating Tuesdays, reaching residents who cannot easily travel to the main site.

title: Margaret Tells Other Seniors: Don't Wait Like I Did
source: 360 Communities – Burnsville Resource Center & Food Shelf

person.name: Margaret
person.bio: Margaret worked in medical records for decades before retiring in Burnsville. She stretches a fixed income with help from the 360 Communities food shelf and now encourages other seniors not to wait.
person.quote: I tell her it's groceries, not charity. You walk in, you fill your cart, and nobody makes you feel small.

bodyRaw:
Margaret was a medical records technician for most of her career: organized, private, and proud of not needing help. When she started visiting the 360 Communities food shelf in Burnsville after retirement, it took her three months to stop apologizing to the volunteers at the door. Now she tells other seniors in her building not to wait the way she did.

She visits once a month, picks her own groceries from a real aisle layout, and makes casseroles that carry her through the week. She has started bringing a neighbor who lives on a similar budget but hadn't worked up the nerve to go alone. "I tell her it's groceries, not charity," Margaret says. "You walk in, you fill your cart, and nobody makes you feel small."

360 Communities Burnsville is at **14750 Cedar Ave S, Suite 100**. Food shelf hours: Tuesday through Friday, 9am to 3pm. Call **952-985-5300**.

title: Rosa Delgado Has Worked the Same Food Shelf for 14 Years

person.name: Rosa Delgado
person.bio: Rosa Delgado has coordinated the Neighbors, Inc. food shelf in South St. Paul since 2012. She expanded distribution days, built local grocery partnerships, and connects every visitor to the full range of Dakota County resources.
person.quote: We stocked these shelves for you. That's the whole point.

bodyRaw:
Rosa Delgado started as a Neighbors, Inc. volunteer in 2012. She didn't plan to stay. Now she's the food shelf coordinator at their South St. Paul site at 166 W. Geneva Ave, and she knows roughly half the families by name.

"The hardest part isn't the work," she says. "It's when families are embarrassed to come back a second time. I always tell them: we stocked these shelves for you. That's the whole point."

Under Rosa's coordination, Neighbors, Inc. expanded from two to three distribution days and launched a standing produce partnership with local grocery stores. She connects first-time visitors to Dakota County emergency assistance, utility help, and affordable housing resources when food insecurity is a symptom of a deeper crisis.

Neighbors, Inc. serves northern Dakota County including South St. Paul, Inver Grove Heights, Mendota Heights, and Sunfish Lake.

title: She's Fed Thousands Without Ever Asking for a Card
source: Interfaith Action of Greater Saint Paul (Native American Food Shelf)

person.name: Fran White Eagle
person.role: Native American Food Shelf Coordinator, Interfaith Action of Greater Saint Paul
person.bio: Fran White Eagle has coordinated the Native American Food Shelf at Interfaith Action for eight years. She has served over 4,200 households and expanded offerings to include traditional foods like wild rice and dried berries.
person.quote: People come in and we treat them like family, because they are family.

bodyRaw:
Fran White Eagle has been coordinating the Native American Food Shelf operated through Interfaith Action of Greater Saint Paul for eight years. Located at 1671 Summit Ave. in the lower level of the building, the food shelf is open Tuesdays and Thursdays, 10am–2pm, and has never required an ID, a tribal enrollment card, or a referral.

"People come in and we treat them like family, because they are family," White Eagle says. "Lakota, Ojibwe, Dakota, urban Indians, people who don't know their tribe. Everyone is welcome here."

The food shelf operates on donated food from Second Harvest Heartland and community drives, supplemented by a large garden maintained by volunteers every summer. White Eagle has expanded the pantry's offerings to include traditional foods (wild rice, fry bread mix, dried berries) alongside the standard staples.

Last year, the food shelf served over 4,200 households. White Eagle wants that number to grow.

title: St. Vinny's on Payne Ave: Thrift Store With a Mission
source: St. Vincent de Paul – Twin Cities (St. Paul)
bodyRaw:
The St. Vincent de Paul thrift store at 789 Payne Ave., St. Paul has been quietly doing two things at once for decades: selling donated goods at prices working families can afford, and using every dollar to fund emergency assistance for neighbors in crisis.

The St. Paul store is one of 11 operated by SVdP Twin Cities, and it is one of the busiest. On any given Saturday morning, the parking lot fills before 9am. Inside, shoppers sort through clothing, housewares, furniture, and electronics. Prices are kept deliberately low: a winter coat for $4.99, a working microwave for $12.

What many shoppers don't realize is that every dollar spent here funds the SVdP assistance program, which provides emergency help for rent, utilities, food, and medicine to anyone who walks into the store and asks. No application required. Neighbors helping neighbors.

title: Theo Bauer Has Rung the Bell Three Years Running
source: The Salvation Army – North Minneapolis (Parkview Worship & Service Center)

person.name: Theo Bauer
person.role: Volunteer Bell Ringer, The Salvation Army
person.bio: Theo Bauer is a retired electrician from Roseville who has volunteered as a Salvation Army bell ringer for three consecutive holiday seasons, logging around 40 hours of shifts at a Target kettle station.
person.quote: You'd be surprised how many people just want someone to say hello.

bodyRaw:
Theo Bauer is not the kind of person who joins committees or wears volunteer T-shirts. He is a retired electrician from Roseville who started ringing bells at the Salvation Army kettle outside a Target in 2023 because his wife signed them both up and he didn't have a good excuse. He has not missed a season since.

He rings two-hour shifts, usually Saturday afternoons, and estimates he has logged around 40 hours across three holiday seasons. He says the best part is not the ringing; it's the conversations. People stop and tell him about their year. Sometimes they tell him things they probably don't tell anyone else. "You'd be surprised how many people just want someone to say hello," Theo says.

The Salvation Army recruits bell ringers every November. No training required, no long-term commitment. Just show up. Sign up at **1000 Penn Ave N, North Minneapolis** or call **612-521-8742**.

title: 11,500 Bell Ringers Braved the Cold This Christmas
source: The Salvation Army – North Minneapolis (Parkview Worship & Service Center)
bodyRaw:
Across Minnesota, more than eleven thousand volunteers picked up a bell and stood outside a store this holiday season. They logged over 26,000 hours between them, in Roseville, Duluth, Rochester, and across the Twin Cities metro. By nearly every measure, it was one of the strongest volunteer turnouts in recent memory.

What makes bell-ringing work is not complexity. There is no training, no background check, no long-term commitment. You show up, ring a bell for an hour or two, and go home. The Salvation Army uses those donations to fund food shelves, utility assistance, youth programs, and transitional housing year-round. The Parkview Center in North Minneapolis alone serves hundreds of families each month with services most people never associate with a red kettle.

The Salvation Army's Parkview Worship and Service Center is at **1000 Penn Ave N, North Minneapolis**. Walk-in services Monday, Wednesday, and Friday, 9am to noon. Call **612-521-8742**.

title: 14,000 Kids Got Gifts Because Strangers Showed Up
source: Community Action Partnership of Hennepin County (CAP-HC)
bodyRaw:
Nearly fourteen thousand children across the Twin Cities received donated toys this past Christmas, and the way it happened matters. Instead of handing out whatever was available, organizers set up client-choice toy shops where parents could walk through and pick the gifts themselves, choosing what their kid would actually love, wrapping it at home, and putting their own name on the tag.

That distinction changes everything. A parent who gets to choose is not receiving charity. They are giving a gift. Community Action Partnership of Hennepin County coordinated several of these distribution sites, working with dozens of toy drive donors, corporate partners, and volunteer sorters to keep the shelves stocked and the lines moving.

CAP-HC serves Hennepin County residents with emergency assistance, housing navigation, and family support programs year-round. Main office: **7051 Brooklyn Blvd, Brooklyn Center**. Call **952-933-9639**.

title: Eviction Notices Are Piling Up. Here's What's Happening
bodyRaw:
Minnesota courts processed more eviction filings last year than at any point since tracking began. The numbers nearly doubled from the pre-pandemic average, and the pace isn't slowing down. Hennepin and Ramsey counties account for the largest share, but suburban courts in Dakota, Scott, and Anoka are seeing sharp increases too. Vacancy rates across the Twin Cities have dropped below three percent, which means families who lose a unit have almost nowhere to go.

HOME Line, the statewide tenant hotline, has been fielding a rising volume of calls from renters who are behind on rent, confused by court notices, or unsure whether their landlord can legally lock them out. Many of the callers have never been through an eviction before. Their tenant counselors explain your rights, help you draft a response to your landlord, and connect you with legal aid when representation is needed. The call is free, and you don't need an ID. Dial **612-728-5767** Monday through Friday, 9am to 4:30pm.

title: Family Kept the Lights On. Here's How
source: 360 Communities – Burnsville Resource Center & Food Shelf
bodyRaw:
Maria Reyes hadn't missed a bill in eleven years. Then a car repair wiped out her savings the same week her hours got cut at work. By the time she called 360 Communities at their Burnsville Resource Center (14750 Cedar Ave, Suite 100), she was ten days from a utility shutoff with two kids at home.

The intake coordinator sat with her for 90 minutes. They filed an emergency utility assistance application, connected her with a food shelf appointment for the following morning, and identified a rental assistance program she hadn't known existed. "I came in sure they couldn't help me," Maria said. "I left with a plan."

360 Communities serves residents of Burnsville, Apple Valley, Eagan, Lakeville, and surrounding communities. Their resource center offers food shelf access, financial coaching, and crisis stabilization, all under one roof. Call 952-985-5300 to schedule.

title: Food Shelf Lines Have Doubled Since 2022
source: The Food Group (Food HelpLine / Fare For All / food bank)
bodyRaw:
Three years ago, most metro food shelves served a predictable roster of regulars. That has changed. Monthly client counts at pantries across Hennepin County have roughly doubled since 2022, and the new visitors look different from who used to walk in. Families with two working parents. Seniors whose fixed incomes stopped covering groceries when prices climbed. People who describe themselves as embarrassed to be asking.

One in four Minnesota families now relies on a food shelf at some point during the year. The Food Group, which operates the statewide Food HelpLine and the Fare For All discount grocery program, has been coordinating supply across dozens of partner pantries to keep shelves stocked. If you need food, call the Food HelpLine at **888-711-1151** (Monday through Friday, 8am to 4:30pm) and they will connect you with the nearest pantry that has capacity. No income verification. No ID required at most locations.

title: From Couch-Surfing to the Courtroom: One Teen's Journey
bodyRaw:
Destiny grew up in the Twin Cities with a mother whose mental illness made stability rare. When her mother left for the West Coast during Destiny's junior year of high school, she was on her own. She couch-surfed through St. Paul for months—sleeping at friends' apartments, eating what was available, trying to keep her grades up while having no fixed address.

A youth shelter connected her with transitional housing, and from that stable ground she did something remarkable: she graduated high school on time. Then Spelman College, cum laude. Then law school in the Twin Cities, where she earned her JD in 2025 and accepted a position as a JAG officer in the U.S. Air Force.

Destiny's story is not about one program. It is about what happens when a young person finds a bed, a meal, and one adult who says "you can do this." People Serving People, the family shelter at **614 S 3rd St, Minneapolis**, provides exactly that for over 100 families every night. If you know a young person who needs shelter, call **612-332-4500**.

title: Halal Food Shelf Fills a Gap No One Else Would
bodyRaw:
When Fadumo Osman moved her family to Burnsville from Minneapolis four years ago, the nearest halal food options were a 40-minute round trip. Generic food shelves helped with pantry staples but couldn't provide the meat and culturally appropriate ingredients her family actually cooked with. She heard about Isuroon from a neighbor.

Isuroon's Burnsville office at 300 E Travelers Trail operates a halal food shelf serving Somali and East African families across the south metro. Beyond groceries, they offer navigation help for benefits, housing referrals, and connections to Somali-speaking case workers who can cut through bureaucratic confusion. "They understand why I'm here and what I actually need," Fadumo said. "That's rare."

The food shelf runs Tuesdays and Thursdays, 10am–2pm. No appointment required. For more information call 952-303-3489.

title: How CLUES Became St. Paul's Living Room
source: CLUES (Comunidades Latinas Unidas En Servicio) – St. Paul
bodyRaw:
On any given Tuesday morning, the lobby at CLUES (Comunidades Latinas Unidas En Servicio) on Minnehaha Avenue looks less like a nonprofit waiting room and more like a reunion. An older woman finishes filling out her food shelf application while chatting with the intake worker in Spanish. Two teenagers review college application essays at a corner table. A father who arrived from Oaxaca three months ago waits for his appointment with an employment counselor.

CLUES has been at this corner of St. Paul since 1981, growing from a small advocacy office into a hub that now serves more than 8,000 individuals a year. The range is remarkable: workforce training, mental health counseling, food access, senior services, citizenship classes, and a thriving arts and culture program. What binds it together is the language: staff communicate in Spanish, English, and Somali, and interpretation is always available.

"People come here because they know they won't have to explain themselves," says Ana Fuentes, a case manager who has worked at CLUES for eleven years. "They can just ask for what they need."

The organization recently opened a second site in Roseville to extend services to the growing Latino population in the northern suburbs, a move that reflects just how much the Twin Cities community has changed since CLUES first opened its doors four decades ago.

title: "I Didn't Know Where to Turn": How One Family Found Help After a House Fire
bodyRaw: When a kitchen fire displaced the Nguyen family from their Brooklyn Center apartment in January, they had no renters insurance and no relatives nearby. With two school-age children and a newborn, Linh Nguyen spent the first night at a hotel paid for by the Red Cross, unsure what would happen next. A case worker at Hennepin County connected the family with CEAP, the Community Emergency Assistance Programs, which has been serving northwest-metro residents since 1970. Within 48 hours, CEAP helped the Nguyens secure temporary housing, provided furniture and household essentials, and connected Linh with a benefits navigator. "They didn't just give us a list of phone numbers," Linh said through an interpreter. "They walked with us through every step. They showed up."

title: Shakopee's Quiet Hunger Problem
bodyRaw:
The CAP Agency food shelf on Vierling Drive doesn't look like a place where 400 families a month come in for help. The waiting room is calm. Staff greet people by name. Kids do homework at a corner table while a parent fills out intake paperwork.

But the numbers are stark. Last year, CAP Scott County distributed over 200,000 pounds of food, up 18 percent from the year before. Most clients are working. Many live in the newer subdivisions along the 169 corridor, places that don't look like they have a food problem from the outside.

"People drive nice cars and still can't make ends meet at the end of the month," said the food shelf coordinator. "The cost of housing out here has outpaced wages. That gap is where we live."

CAP accepts walk-ins with no ID required. Families can visit once per month and choose their own groceries from a client-choice model, not pre-boxed bags. The agency also connects clients to utility assistance, eviction prevention, and job training on-site.

Volunteers are the backbone. CAP needs 15 to 20 people per week to sort and stock shelves, greet clients, and help with mobile pantry deliveries to Jordan and Prior Lake. Shifts run Tuesday through Friday, 8:30 a.m. to noon.

title: She Worked Her Whole Life. Now She Eats Twice a Day
source: 360 Communities – Burnsville Resource Center & Food Shelf
bodyRaw:
Margaret spent decades working in medical records at a hospital south of the metro. She retired at 72 because her body told her to, not because the math worked out. Her Social Security check barely covers rent and utilities in her Burnsville apartment, so she made adjustments. Two meals a day instead of three. Driving only when absolutely necessary. Skipping the things most people don't think twice about.

Once a month, she drives four miles to the 360 Communities food shelf in Burnsville, where she fills a cart with vegetables, frozen proteins, and canned goods, her pick, not a pre-packed box. She makes casseroles that stretch across the week. She is deeply grateful to the volunteers who stock those shelves and the donors who fund them. But she will also tell you, quietly, that she never expected to be here.

360 Communities' Burnsville Resource Center is at **14750 Cedar Ave S, Suite 100**. The food shelf is open Tuesday through Friday, 9am to 3pm. Call **952-985-5300** to schedule or walk in during hours.

title: Valley Outreach Kept the Lights On

A neighbor told her about Valley Outreach in Stillwater, and she drove over that afternoon. Within two hours she had a utility assistance check and a bag of groceries. Six months later she is a regular volunteer, sorting produce on Thursday mornings. "I wanted to give back before I even stopped needing help," she said. Valley Outreach served more than 4,200 households in Washington County last year, distributing over 700,000 pounds of food and connecting families with emergency financial assistance for rent, utilities, and medical bills.

title: What Karen Families Found When They Stayed
bodyRaw:
When Paw Htoo arrived in St. Paul in 2009, she spoke no English and knew no one outside her resettlement cohort. Her caseworker at the time handed her a bus map and a list of appointments. She made every one of them, and then she kept showing up, long after the requirements ended, to help the families who arrived after her.

Seventeen years later, Htoo works as a community health navigator for the Karen Organization of Minnesota, which is based on University Avenue and serves roughly 2,500 Karen and Karenni community members in the metro. The organization began as an informal meeting group in the early 2000s and has grown into a full social services agency offering English classes, employment support, health navigation, cultural programming, and emergency financial assistance.

What keeps families here, Htoo says, is not just services; it is people. "When your neighbor speaks your language and knows what the first winter felt like, you stay."

KOM's health navigation program, funded in part through a partnership with Regions Hospital, has become a model studied by resettlement organizations in other states. Community members serve as frontline navigators, translating not just language but context, helping families understand how the U.S. healthcare system works and what questions to ask.

title: Brooklyn Park Program Helped 200 Immigrants Land Jobs
source: CAPI USA (Communities Advancing Prosperity for Immigrants)
bodyRaw: CAPI USA's employment team in Brooklyn Park placed more than 200 job seekers into stable positions last year, most of them recent immigrants and refugees. The program pairs each participant with a bilingual job coach who helps with applications, interview practice, and workplace culture questions that never come up in a job listing. Many participants arrive speaking limited English and leave with a paycheck and growing confidence. CAPI also runs English classes, a food shelf, and financial coaching under one roof, so people can handle several needs in a single visit.

The shelf is now open Monday through Thursday from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m., up from two days a week last year. Visitors choose their own groceries from a selection that includes halal meat, fresh vegetables, rice, and cooking oils. No ID is needed on your first visit. The center also runs youth programs and community space, so families can handle multiple errands in one stop.

title: Scott County Emergency Funds Helped 1,400 Families
source: Scott County Financial Assistance (Crisis Funds / Emergency General Assistance)
bodyRaw: When a car breaks down or a medical bill lands in the same month as rent, Scott County's emergency financial assistance program can step in fast. Last year the program helped more than 1,400 households keep the lights on, cover a security deposit, or fill a prescription they could not afford. Applications go through Scott County Human Services and most decisions come within a few business days. You do not need to be on any other program to qualify. Bring proof of income, a lease or mortgage statement, and an ID. Staff can walk you through the rest.

title: Free Immigration Legal Clinic in St. Paul March 18
source: The Advocates for Human Rights (Immigrant Client Line)
bodyRaw: The Advocates for Human Rights is holding a free immigration legal clinic on Wednesday, March 18, from 5 to 8 p.m. at a community site in St. Paul. Volunteer attorneys will offer one-on-one consultations on asylum cases, DACA renewals, family petitions, and other immigration questions. Interpreters will be available for Spanish, Somali, and Oromo. No appointment necessary, but arriving early is recommended since slots fill quickly. Bring any paperwork you have, including court notices, work permits, and ID. The clinic is open to anyone regardless of status.

title: Teresa Lopez Builds Careers One Resume at a Time

person.name: Teresa Lopez
person.bio: Teresa came to Minnesota from Guatemala at nineteen and found her footing through a workforce program. She has spent six years at CLUES helping Latino job seekers build resumes and land stable work.
person.quote: A good job changes everything. Not just the money. The way you carry yourself.

bodyRaw: Teresa Lopez has spent six years at CLUES helping Latino job seekers in Minneapolis find work that fits their skills. She came to Minnesota from Guatemala at nineteen, landed her first job through a workforce program, and decided to pay it forward. Today she coaches about 80 people a year through resume writing, mock interviews, and employer introductions. Many of her clients arrive nervous and leave with a plan. The CareerForce Center at CLUES is one of the few places in the metro where the entire process happens in Spanish.

title: Deb Hartmann Turned a Closet into a Community Lifeline

person.name: Deb Hartmann
person.bio: Deb has run the Peace Center Food Shelf in New Prague for eleven years, growing it from a closet-sized pantry to a full weekly operation with a mobile route and thirty regular volunteers.
person.quote: The hardest part is getting someone to walk through the door the first time. After that, they know they belong.

bodyRaw: Eleven years ago the Peace Center Food Shelf in New Prague fit inside a single closet at the back of a church. Deb Hartmann took it over and built it into a weekly operation with thirty volunteers, a mobile pantry that reaches rural Scott County, and a walk-in shelf stocked with fresh produce, dairy, and meat. She knows most visitors by name and makes a point of asking how the kids are doing. The food shelf now serves around 200 households a month, many of them families who drive twenty minutes or more from the surrounding countryside.
